site.name: Backurus
site.tagline: A modern backend framework for Node.js inspired by Laravel.
site.footer: Backurus documentation. Build fast, structured APIs with modern JavaScript.

search.introduction_desc: Overview, architecture, workflow, and core Backurus features.
search.installation_desc: Install dependencies, configure the environment, and boot the app.
search.quickstart_desc: Generate files, migrate, seed, and run your first API.
search.routing_desc: Expressive route syntax, middleware aliases, and route inspection.
search.controllers_desc: Keep controllers thin and return built-in response helpers.
search.models_desc: Model CRUD, query builder, pagination, eager loading, and soft deletes.
search.migrations_desc: Schema builder methods, modifiers, foreign keys, and migration commands.

search.authentication_desc: JWT auth flow, protected routes, and authorization patterns.

search.plugins_desc: Plugins, service container access, and facades.

home.title: Backurus makes Node.js API development feel familiar, fast, and disciplined.
home.lead: Backurus brings together `node urus`, expressive routing, an Eloquent-style ORM, migrations, validation, JWT auth, queue jobs, scheduling, plugins, facades, and a multilingual docs site.
home.panel_body: Backurus is designed for teams that want Laravel-like backend ergonomics with a modern JavaScript runtime and a direct API-first workflow.
home.card_one_body: Generate controllers, models, requests, migrations, policies, resources, and modules with one command.
home.card_two_body: Use CRUD helpers, fluent queries, pagination, relationships, and eager loading without excessive ceremony.
home.card_three_body: JWT auth, middleware aliases, hot reload, config caching, queues, scheduling, plugins, and WebSockets are already built in.
home.section_one_body: Backurus is a backend framework for Node.js focused on building APIs quickly without sacrificing structure. Its layout is opinionated enough to keep teams aligned, but still lightweight enough for small services and internal tools.
home.section_two_body: Application code lives in `app/`, HTTP definitions live in `routes/`, framework internals live in `core/`, migrations and seeders live in `database/`, and the documentation website lives in `docs/`.
home.section_three_item_one: REST APIs with route strings such as `UserController@index` and middleware aliases such as `auth`.
home.section_three_item_two: Database-backed resources with validation, migrations, seeders, resources, and policies.
home.section_three_item_three: Realtime and background features with queues, scheduling, WebSockets, plugins, and facades.

installation.title: Installation
installation.lead: Install Backurus, configure your environment, and prepare the database driver before running migrations.
installation.section_one_body: Use Node.js 20 or newer. Backurus currently supports MySQL and SQLite through its built-in database manager.
installation.section_two_body: The intended installation flow is `npx create-backurus-app my-api`, then `cd my-api` and `node urus serve`. For this repository scaffold, run `npm install`, configure `.env`, then execute the migrate and seed commands.
installation.section_three_body: Backurus loads `.env` automatically. Typical keys include `APP_NAME`, `APP_PORT`, `DB_CONNECTION`, `DB_HOST`, `DB_PORT`, `DB_DATABASE`, `DB_USERNAME`, `DB_PASSWORD`, `JWT_SECRET`, and `QUEUE_CONNECTION`.
installation.section_three_item_two: Use `DB_CONNECTION=sqlite` and point `DB_DATABASE` to a local file for SQLite.
installation.section_three_item_three: Framework config is read from `config/app.js`, `config/database.js`, `config/auth.js`, and `config/queue.js`.

quickstart.title: Quick Start
quickstart.lead: The fastest path is generate, migrate, seed, and serve.
quickstart.section_one_body: Backurus ships with default auth endpoints, health checks, sample resources, response helpers, queue hooks, and route listing support. Start with the generator commands, then build the database and serve the app.
quickstart.section_two_item_two: `POST /register` creates a user and emits an event.
quickstart.section_two_item_four: `GET /products` returns the sample resource with pagination metadata.
quickstart.section_three_body: Use `node urus migrate`, `node urus db:seed`, and `node urus serve`. If you need a full reset, run `node urus migrate:fresh`.

routing.title: Routing
routing.lead: Backurus routing stays close to Laravel: simple definitions, controller action strings, route names, and middleware chaining.
routing.section_one_body: Use `Route.get('/users', 'UserController@index')`, `Route.post('/users', 'UserController@store')`, and similar route definitions in `routes/api.js` or `routes/web.js`. Route files are loaded automatically.
routing.section_two_body: Chain `.middleware('auth')` to apply a middleware alias and `.name('users.index')` to attach a stable route name used by tooling and inspection output.
routing.section_three_body: Run `node urus route:list` to print the method, URI, action, and route name for every registered route.

controllers.title: Controllers
controllers.lead: Controllers should stay thin and focus on request orchestration.
controllers.section_one_body: Backurus resolves controller methods from route strings like `ProductController@index`. Controllers should read request input, call validation or models, and return response helpers.
controllers.section_three_item_one: Put validation rules in request classes.
controllers.section_three_item_two: Move long-running work into jobs or services.
controllers.section_three_item_three: Use resources when response payloads need consistent transformation.

models.title: ORM & Models
models.lead: Backurus models expose an Eloquent-style API for CRUD, fluent querying, relationships, pagination, eager loading, timestamps, and soft deletes.
models.section_one_body: Use `all`, `find`, `create`, `update`, and `delete` on model classes for the most common operations.
models.section_three_body: Available relationship helpers are `hasOne`, `hasMany`, `belongsTo`, and `belongsToMany`. Use `with('posts')` to fetch related data in the same query workflow.
models.section_four_body: Enable `static softDeletes = true` to use `deleted_at`. When timestamps are enabled, `created_at` and `updated_at` are managed automatically.

migrations.title: Migrations
migrations.lead: Backurus migrations use a schema builder DSL with familiar table methods, modifiers, and foreign keys.
migrations.section_two_item_one: `node urus migrate`, `node urus migrate:rollback`, `node urus migrate:reset`.
migrations.section_two_item_three: `node urus db:seed` to restore baseline data after schema changes.

validation.title: Validation
validation.lead: Backurus request validation uses simple rule classes and automatic 422 JSON responses.
validation.section_one_body: Declare rules in request classes using pipe syntax such as `required|min:3`, `required|email`, and `required|min:6`.
validation.section_three_body: When validation fails, Backurus returns a structured `422` response with field-specific error details.

authentication.title: Authentication
authentication.lead: Backurus ships with JWT authentication, default login and register endpoints, and an `auth` middleware alias.
authentication.section_one_body: `AuthController@login` signs a JWT, `AuthController@register` creates a user and returns a token, and `/profile` demonstrates a protected route.
authentication.section_two_body: Apply `.middleware('auth')` to secure a route. Backurus reads the bearer token, verifies it, and assigns the authenticated payload to `req.user`.
authentication.section_three_body: Use the `Gate` facade or guards like `roleGuard('admin')` when route access depends on roles or ownership.

queue.title: Queue & Schedule
queue.lead: Backurus supports background jobs, delayed dispatch, queue workers, restart signals, and a simple scheduler.
queue.section_one_body: Jobs are plain classes. Dispatch them with `queue.dispatch(job)` or `queue.dispatchLater(job, delayMs)`.
queue.section_two_body: Use `node urus queue:work` to run workers and `node urus queue:restart` to signal long-running workers to restart cleanly.
queue.section_three_body: Define scheduled commands in `routes/console.js` with `Schedule.command('route:list').daily()` and run them with `node urus schedule:run`.

websocket.title: WebSocket
websocket.lead: Backurus includes a built-in WebSocket server at `/ws` for realtime notifications and live updates.
websocket.section_one_body: The WebSocket hub attaches to the same HTTP server instance, so API traffic and realtime traffic share one runtime.
websocket.section_two_body: Use `ws.emit('notification', data)` from controllers, jobs, or event listeners.
websocket.section_three_body: Use the built-in hub for dashboards, notifications, activity feeds, and collaborative features.

plugins.title: Plugins & Facades
plugins.lead: Backurus auto-loads plugins and exposes a small facade layer for common framework services.
plugins.section_one_body: Drop plugin folders into `plugins/` and export a default bootstrap function. Plugins receive access to router, events, queue, container, config, and the WebSocket hub.
plugins.section_three_body: Use plugins for boot-time registration, observability, logging, or analytics. Use facades when a compact API improves readability.

cli.title: CLI Commands
cli.lead: Everything in Backurus runs through `node urus`.
cli.section_three_body: Use generators first, then migrate, seed, inspect routes, and finally serve the app. This is the fastest path to a new API module in Backurus.
